title: Bullous Pemphigoid
oneLiner: An autoimmune subepidermal blistering disease in elderly patients causing tense, pruritic bullae on flexural skin; early recognition prevents infection and steroid overuse.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 78-year-old woman with hypertension and mild dementia presents to the ED with a 3-week history of intensely itchy blisters. She first noticed red, itchy patches on her inner thighs and forearms, which then developed into large, fluid-filled blisters that don't pop easily. On examination, you see tense bullae up to 3 cm on flexural surfaces (axillae, groins, antecubital fossae), some with surrounding urticarial plaques. No oral mucosal lesions. She is systemically well, no fever, but looks miserable from the itch.
- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Age 78* → Bullous pemphigoid peaks in 70–80s; any elderly with new pruritic bullae until proven otherwise.
  - *Intensely itchy first* → Pruritus is the earliest symptom, often preceding blisters by weeks → "prodromal pruritic phase".
  - *Tense blisters that don't pop easily* → Subepidermal split; roof is thicker than intraepidermal blisters (pemphigus).
  - *Flexural distribution (axillae, groins, antecubitals)* → Classic sites; also lower abdomen, inner thighs.
  - *No oral mucosal lesions* → Very helpful: mucosal involvement is rare (≤20%) and usually mild; contrast with pemphigus where oral lesions are near-universal.
  - *Surrounding urticarial plaques* → Active inflammation; may be misdiagnosed as urticaria initially.
  - *Systemically well* → Unlike Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis where patient is toxic.
- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - *Eczematous variant*: Only itchy, dry patches without blisters for weeks — "prebullous pemphigoid". Often treated as eczema until blisters appear.
  - *Localized variant*: Blisters confined to one site (e.g., only lower legs, only groins) mimicking cellulitis or irritant dermatitis.
  - *Urticarial variant*: Persistent urticaria-like plaques without clear blisters; pruritus dominant. Biopsy may reveal early pemphigoid.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Bullous pemphigoid is an autoimmune blistering skin disease where your body's immune system attacks the glue that holds the top layer of skin (epidermis) to the layer below (dermis), causing tense, fluid-filled blisters on skin that is often already itchy and red.
- **Why it matters clinically:**
  - *Frequency*: Most common autoimmune blistering disease in Western populations; incidence 2–14 per 100,000 over age 70. As population ages, you will see it.
  - *Stakes*: While rarely fatal itself (mortality ~20–40% in elderly due to comorbidities), untreated disease leads to widespread skin breakdown → secondary infection, sepsis, fluid/electrolyte loss, and profound itching that destroys quality of life.
  - *Common pitfalls*: Misdiagnosing as eczema, urticaria, or drug reaction → delays appropriate steroid therapy. Conversely, over-treating mild cases with high-dose systemic steroids causes avoidable harm (diabetes, osteoporosis, infections).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The story:**
  1. In healthy skin, **hemidesmosomes** are protein complexes that anchor the basal layer of the epidermis to the basement membrane. Two key proteins in hemidesmosomes are **BP180 (type XVII collagen)** and **BP230 (bullous pemphigoid antigen 1)**.
  2. In bullous pemphigoid, autoreactive B cells produce IgG autoantibodies (mainly IgG1 and IgG4) against BP180's non-collagenous NC16A domain (dominant epitope) and less often against BP230.
  3. These antibodies bind to the hemidesmosomes, triggering complement activation (C3 deposition) and recruitment of inflammatory cells — especially **eosinophils** — which release proteolytic enzymes (matrix metalloproteinases) that digest the basement membrane.
  4. Result: the epidermis lifts off the dermis → a subepidermal split → fluid accumulates → **tense blister**.
- **WHY each clinical sign happens:**
  - *Tense bullae* → Split below the epidermis; the roof is full-thickness epidermis which is strong, so blisters don't rupture easily (contrast with pemphigus where split is intraepidermal → fragile blisters).
  - *Pruritus* → Eosinophil degranulation releases histamine, eosinophil peroxidase, and other mediators that stimulate itch nerves. Often pruritus precedes blisters because immune activation begins before basement membrane cleavage.
  - *Urticarial plaques* → Complement-driven inflammation with vasodilation and edema in the dermis; these are "pre-bullous" zones where the split hasn't fully developed.
  - *Flexural distribution* → Not fully understood, but areas of friction/pressure may trigger blister formation in pre-sensitized skin. Also, lower body temperature in flexures may slow healing.
  - *Rarity of mucosal lesions* → Mucosal hemidesmosomes contain similar proteins but with slightly different isoforms (e.g., BP180 is present but may be less accessible to antibodies due to different splice variants). Also, the oral mucosa has faster healing and different immune milieu.

- **Connect pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**
  - Patient's pruritus before blisters → prodromal phase due to early eosinophil infiltration.
  - Tense blisters that don't pop → subepidermal split.
  - Surrounding urticarial plaques → complement activation and dermal edema.
  - No mucosal involvement → typical of BP; if present, think of mucous membrane pemphigoid instead.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Causes / Triggers | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common, >80%) | No identifiable trigger | "Elderly with no new medications" |
| **Drug-induced** | Diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide), ACE inhibitors (captopril, lisinopril), penicillamine, antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins), NSAIDs | "Blistering started 2–4 weeks after starting new drug" |
| **Neurological association** | Parkinson's disease, dementia, multiple sclerosis, stroke, epilepsy | "Elderly with bradykinesia, tremor, or cognitive decline" |
| **Physical triggers** | UV radiation, radiotherapy, trauma, burns | "Blisters confined to irradiated skin field" |
| **Viral** | Rare: HSV, VZV, HHV-8 (implicated in a few cases) | "Blistering erupted after oral herpes outbreak" |
| **Vaccination** | Influenza, pneumococcal (very rare case reports) | "Onset within 2 weeks of vaccination" |

- **Most common cause:** Idiopathic — no trigger found in the vast majority. Drug-induced is the most important to screen for because withdrawal can lead to resolution.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single validated scoring system in clinical use; diagnosis relies on **clinical picture + histology + immunofluorescence**. The combination is gold standard.
- **For each test:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Skin biopsy for histology** (punch biopsy of *new* blister, including edge) | Subepidermal split with eosinophil-rich infiltrate in the dermis (eosinophilic spongiosis) | Confirm level of blister and presence of eosinophils — key distinguishing feature from pemphigus (intraepidermal split) and linear IgA (neutrophil-rich) | Subepidermal split + eosinophils = consistent with BP; if neutrophils dominate, think linear IgA or dermatitis herpetiformis |
| **Direct immunofluorescence (DIF)** on perilesional skin (normal-appearing skin within 1 cm of blister) | Linear deposition of IgG and C3 along the dermal-epidermal junction | Definitive diagnostic test: shows where the immune attack is happening | Linear IgG + C3 at basement membrane = diagnostic of bullous pemphigoid (or mucous membrane pemphigoid if mucosa involved) |
| **Indirect immunofluorescence (IIF)** on monkey esophagus or salt-split skin (patient serum tested) | Circulating anti-basement membrane antibodies | Confirm active disease; also used to monitor response to therapy; salt-split skin shows antibody binding to *epidermal* side (roof) | Positive on epidermal side = BP; binding to dermal side = epidermolysis bullosa acquisita |
| **ELISA for BP180 and BP230** | Quantify autoantibody levels (anti-BP180 NC16A and anti-BP230) | Highly sensitive (90%+) and specific; aids diagnosis and predicts relapse | High titers correlate with active disease; falling titers with treatment; rising titers predict flare |
| **Full blood count** | Eosinophilia (often >0.5 ×10⁹/L) | Eosinophils are effector cells; high counts correlate with disease activity | Eosinophilia + tense blisters = strongly suggestive of BP |
| **Skin swab for culture** | Infecting organism if blister fluid purulent or surrounding cellulitis | Rule out secondary infection (Staph. aureus, Group A Strep) before starting high-dose steroids | Positive culture should prompt antibiotic therapy |

- **Single finding that confirms:** **Linear IgG and C3 along the dermal-epidermal junction on DIF** — this is pathognomonic.
- **Single finding that rules out:** **Intraepidermal split (acantholysis) on histology** → pemphigus vulgaris, not pemphigoid.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management depends on extent and severity. Use the **modified BP Disease Area Index (BPDAI)** to guide initial therapy:
- **Mild:** <10% body surface area (BSA) blisters/erosions; can use topical steroids.
- **Moderate:** 10–30% BSA; need systemic therapy.
- **Severe:** >30% BSA or rapid progression; high-dose systemic steroids + steroid-sparing agent.

- **Immediate (first hour):**
  - **What:** Assess airway, breathing, circulation. Is the patient septic? Any mucosal involvement? Check for Nikolsky sign (lateral pressure on blister → extension in pemphigus, NOT in BP). Obtain urgent skin biopsy (punch of new blister + perilesional for DIF). Send FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP, BP180 ELISA.
  - **Why:** Rule out SJS/TEN (Nikolsky positive, target lesions, systemic toxicity). Biopsy before starting steroids (steroids can clear DIF). Baseline bloods to check for drug-induced causes and monitor later.
  - **Doses:** None yet — focus on diagnosis and assessment.

- **Ward (24–72h):**
  - **Mild disease:** **Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream** applied twice daily to affected areas, including surrounding erythema, for 2–4 weeks. This is as effective as systemic steroids in mild-moderate BP with fewer side effects. If refractory, add **doxycycline 100 mg PO BID** plus **nicotinamide 500 mg PO TID** — this combination has good evidence (BLISTER trial) and fewer side effects than prednisolone.
  - **Moderate–severe:** **Prednisolone** starting dose 0.5–1 mg/kg/day PO (max 80 mg/day) for 2–4 weeks, then taper slowly (by 5–10 mg every 2 weeks) aiming for maintenance ≤10 mg/day. Higher doses (1 mg/kg) for severe but start low if possible to reduce toxicity.
  - **Why prednisolone?** Rapidly suppresses inflammation and antibody production. Taper slowly because disease flares on fast tapering.
  - **Steoid-sparing agent** added early in moderate-severe disease: **Mycophenolate mofetil** 500–1000 mg BID PO or **Azathioprine** 1–2 mg/kg/day PO (check TPMT level first). These take 4–8 weeks to work, so don't stop prednisolone early.
  - **Wound care:** Puncture and drain large blisters aseptically (keep roof intact as biological dressing). Apply paraffin gauze (Jelonet) to eroded areas. Topical antimicrobials (silver sulfadiazine) if signs of infection.
  - **Monitoring:** Daily skin assessment for new blisters, signs of infection (purulent discharge, surrounding erythema, fever); FBC, CRP, U&Es weekly.

- **Long-term:**
  - **What:** Taper prednisolone as above; maintain steroid-sparing agent for 6–12 months. Once disease controlled (no new blisters for 2–4 weeks), slowly taper off immunosuppressants.
  - **Why:** BP is chronic but self-limiting in many; average duration 2–5 years. Relapse common during taper, so do it slowly. Continue topical steroids as needed for minor flares.
  - **Doses:** Maintenance prednisolone 2.5–5 mg/day; mycophenolate mofetil 500–1000 mg BID.
  - **Fallback:** **Rituximab** (1 g IV day 1 and day 15) for refractory disease; blocking B cells reduces antibody production.
  - **Prevent osteoporosis:** All patients on >7.5 mg/day prednisolone for >3 months need calcium/vitamin D supplements and bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate 70 mg once weekly).

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - **Don't start high-dose prednisolone before confirming diagnosis** — if it's SJS/TEN, steroids may worsen outcome. If it's drug-induced BP, withdrawing the drug may be enough.
  - **Don't ignore oral lesions** — if present, consider mucous membrane pemphigoid or pemphigus; need ophthalmology referral for conjunctival involvement.
  - **Don't use systemic steroids alone for mild disease** — the BLISTER trial showed topical clobetasol is as effective and safer.
  - **Don't forget to check for underlying malignancy** — only if atypical (young age, recalcitrant disease, associated with paraneoplastic pemphigus? Actually BP is NOT typically paraneoplastic; screening not routinely recommended unless red flags).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Bullous Pemphigoid | Pemphigus Vulgaris | Dermatitis Herpetiformis | Linear IgA Disease | Epidermolysis Bullosa Acquisita |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Blisters** | Tense, intact easily | Flaccid, fragile, easily ruptured | Small tense blisters on extensor surfaces | Tense blisters, often annular, "string of pearls" | Tense, trauma-prone (milia, scarring) |
| **Mucosal involvement** | Rare (<20%, mild) | Near-universal (oral, genital, conjunctival) | None (oral lesions rare) | Oral / ocular / genital in 50% | Oral and esophageal common |
| **Pruritus** | Intense, often pre-bullous | Mild or absent | Extremely intense, burning | Variable, can be pruritic | Mild |
| **Nikolsky sign** | Negative (blister doesn't extend) | Positive (lateral pressure extends blister) | Negative | Negative | Negative (occasionally positive in scarred areas) |
| **Histology** | Subepidermal split, eosinophils | Intraepidermal split (acantholysis), neutrophils/ eosinophils | Subepidermal microabscesses with neutrophils at dermal papillae | Subepidermal with neutrophils | Subepidermal, neutrophils, fibrosis |
| **Direct immunofluorescence** | Linear IgG + C3 at basement membrane | Intercellular (fishnet) IgG ± C3 in epidermis | Granular IgA in dermal papillae | Linear IgA at basement membrane | Linear IgG + C3 at basement membrane (dermal side on salt-split) |
| **Associated condition** | Parkinson's, dementia, drug-induced | Mostly idiopathic, some drugs | Gluten-sensitive enteropathy (celiac) | Vancomycin, other drugs; idiopathic | Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's) |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Pruritus + tense blisters + flexural distribution + elderly | Painful oral erosions + flaccid skin blisters | Severe itch + gluten sensitivity + extensor distribution | "String of pearls" annular lesions + drug history | Trauma-induced blisters + milia + scarring + IBD |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Secondary bacterial infection (most common):** Pustules, spreading erythema, purulent exudate, fever. **What to do:** skin swab, start empiric antibiotics (flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS PO) and adjust based on culture. If neglected → cellulitis, sepsis, bacteraemia.
- **Sepsis:** Widespread skin breakdown → loss of barrier → bacteria enter bloodstream. **Signs:** Fever, tachycardia, hypotension, high NEWS2, raised lactate. **What to do:** IV fluids, urgent blood cultures, IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe), escalate to medical team, consider ITU if septic shock.
- **Fluid and electrolyte imbalance:** Large eroded surfaces lose protein-rich exudate → hypoalbuminaemia, hypoproteinaemic edema. **Signs:** Pitting edema, low albumin (<25 g/L). **What to do:** monitor albumin, replace with IV fluids if needed (crystalloid), consider nutritional support (high protein). Rare unless >30% BSA.
- **Steroid-related complications (iatrogenic):** Hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, avascular necrosis, cataracts, immunosuppression → opportunistic infections (PCP, CMV, TB) if high-dose prolonged. **Prevention:** check blood glucose, start oral hypoglycaemics if needed; PCP prophylaxis (co-trimoxazole 480 mg OD) if prednisolone >20 mg/day for >1 month; bone protection as above.
- **Late presentation if untreated:** Months of severe pruritus and widespread blisters → chronic wounds → contractures, scarring (unusual but possible), depression, social withdrawal. In rare cases, bullous pemphigoid can blister extensively enough to be fatal from sepsis/multi-organ failure.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for classic features: "BP in the ELDERLY"**
  - **E** = Elderly (age >70)
  - **L** = Linear IgG + C3 on DIF
  - **D** = Dermal-epidermal split (subepidermal)
  - **E** = Eosinophils on histology
  - **R** = Rare mucosal involvement
  - **L** = Long pruritic prodrome
  - **Y** = Yes, treat with steroids

- **Mnemonic for triggers: "DAN UP"**
  - **D** = Drugs (diuretics, ACEi, penicillamine)
  - **A** = Associated neurological (Parkinson's, dementia)
  - **N** = Nothing (idiopathic, most common)
  - **U** = UV / radiation
  - **P** = Physical trauma

- **High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
  1. Any elderly patient with unexplained itch >2 weeks → think pre-bullous pemphigoid. Do a skin biopsy for DIF even if no blisters.
  2. Tense blister + flexural distribution + eosinophilia = bullous pemphigoid until proven otherwise.
  3. Oral involvement is rare; if present and severe, reconsider diagnosis (pemphigus or mucous membrane pemphigoid).
  4. The "string of pearls" sign (annular grouping of blisters) is classic for **linear IgA disease**, not bullous pemphigoid — don't confuse them.
  5. **Doxycycline + nicotinamide** is a safe, steroid-sparing alternative to systemic steroids for mild-moderate BP — recommend it frequently to avoid steroid side effects in elderly.
  6. Relapse is common during first 6 months after tapering; do not stop all treatment too soon. Consider slow taper over 12 months.
  7. Monitoring anti-BP180 ELISA titers can predict relapse: a 4-fold drop correlates with remission; a rise precedes flare by 4–6 weeks.

- **Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
  - Mistaking Nikolsky-positive pemphigus for BP → delays correct treatment (pemphigus needs higher-dose steroids + steroid-sparing early).
  - Starting systemic steroids before DIF results → can clear immune deposits and make diagnosis impossible.
  - Using only topical steroids on severe disease → progression to >30% BSA and sepsis.
  - Not checking for underlying drug cause → persisting trigger until drug removed.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Condition | When to Call Senior / ICU | Specific Thresholds |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Widespread involvement (>30% BSA)** | Call dermatology registrar / consultant for joint management | Blisters cover >30% of body surface area; risk of sepsis, fluid loss, hypercatabolism |
| **Suspected sepsis** | Call medical registrar / sepsis team; consider ITU | **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or **lactate > 2 mmol/L**; fever >38.5°C or hypothermia <36°C; systolic BP <90 mmHg; new confusion |
| **Mucosal involvement with respiratory / ophthalmic signs** | Call ENT/ophthalmology and senior medics | Hoarseness, stridor, dysphagia → airway compromise; conjunctival injection, synechiae → blindness risk |
| **Poor response to first-line therapy** (no improvement after 2 weeks of prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg) | Call dermatology consultant for second-line options | Persistent new blister formation despite therapy; rising anti-BP180 titers |
| **Steroid toxicity** | Endocrine / rheumatology input | Random blood glucose >15 mmol/L (steroid-induced diabetes); new avascular necrosis (hip pain); active tuberculosis/reactivation of latent infection |
| **Anaphylaxis / drug reaction to treatment** | Immediate crash team call | Angioedema, stridor, hypotension, urticaria following rituximab or antibiotic; treat with 0.5 mg IM adrenaline |

- **Key numerical thresholds to remember:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** → call for urgent review; likely sepsis in this context.
  - **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** → tissue hypoperfusion; escalate to ITU if >4.
  - **GCS < 13** or new confusion → possible cerebral oedema from sepsis or steroid-induced hyperglycaemia; call senior.
  - **Platelets < 50 × 10⁹/L** or **INR > 1.5** → possible DIC from sepsis; call haematology and ITU.

---

*Final thought: Bullous pemphigoid is a great "syndrome" to master because it teaches you how to think about autoimmune blistering diseases: pattern of blister, depth of split, immunofluorescence, and age. Always ask yourself: "Could this be drug-induced?" Then treat the skin while protecting the patient from steroids.*

rawMarkdown:
# Bullous Pemphigoid

**One-liner:** An autoimmune subepidermal blistering disease in elderly patients causing tense, pruritic bullae on flexural skin; early recognition prevents infection and steroid overuse.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

- **Classic vignette:** A 78-year-old woman with hypertension and mild dementia presents to the ED with a 3-week history of intensely itchy blisters. She first noticed red, itchy patches on her inner thighs and forearms, which then developed into large, fluid-filled blisters that don't pop easily. On examination, you see tense bullae up to 3 cm on flexural surfaces (axillae, groins, antecubital fossae), some with surrounding urticarial plaques. No oral mucosal lesions. She is systemically well, no fever, but looks miserable from the itch.
- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Age 78* → Bullous pemphigoid peaks in 70–80s; any elderly with new pruritic bullae until proven otherwise.
  - *Intensely itchy first* → Pruritus is the earliest symptom, often preceding blisters by weeks → "prodromal pruritic phase".
  - *Tense blisters that don't pop easily* → Subepidermal split; roof is thicker than intraepidermal blisters (pemphigus).
  - *Flexural distribution (axillae, groins, antecubitals)* → Classic sites; also lower abdomen, inner thighs.
  - *No oral mucosal lesions* → Very helpful: mucosal involvement is rare (≤20%) and usually mild; contrast with pemphigus where oral lesions are near-universal.
  - *Surrounding urticarial plaques* → Active inflammation; may be misdiagnosed as urticaria initially.
  - *Systemically well* → Unlike Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis where patient is toxic.
- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - *Eczematous variant*: Only itchy, dry patches without blisters for weeks — "prebullous pemphigoid". Often treated as eczema until blisters appear.
  - *Localized variant*: Blisters confined to one site (e.g., only lower legs, only groins) mimicking cellulitis or irritant dermatitis.
  - *Urticarial variant*: Persistent urticaria-like plaques without clear blisters; pruritus dominant. Biopsy may reveal early pemphigoid.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

- **Plain-language definition:** Bullous pemphigoid is an autoimmune blistering skin disease where your body's immune system attacks the glue that holds the top layer of skin (epidermis) to the layer below (dermis), causing tense, fluid-filled blisters on skin that is often already itchy and red.
- **Why it matters clinically:**
  - *Frequency*: Most common autoimmune blistering disease in Western populations; incidence 2–14 per 100,000 over age 70. As population ages, you will see it.
  - *Stakes*: While rarely fatal itself (mortality ~20–40% in elderly due to comorbidities), untreated disease leads to widespread skin breakdown → secondary infection, sepsis, fluid/electrolyte loss, and profound itching that destroys quality of life.
  - *Common pitfalls*: Misdiagnosing as eczema, urticaria, or drug reaction → delays appropriate steroid therapy. Conversely, over-treating mild cases with high-dose systemic steroids causes avoidable harm (diabetes, osteoporosis, infections).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

- **The story:**
  1. In healthy skin, **hemidesmosomes** are protein complexes that anchor the basal layer of the epidermis to the basement membrane. Two key proteins in hemidesmosomes are **BP180 (type XVII collagen)** and **BP230 (bullous pemphigoid antigen 1)**.
  2. In bullous pemphigoid, autoreactive B cells produce IgG autoantibodies (mainly IgG1 and IgG4) against BP180's non-collagenous NC16A domain (dominant epitope) and less often against BP230.
  3. These antibodies bind to the hemidesmosomes, triggering complement activation (C3 deposition) and recruitment of inflammatory cells — especially **eosinophils** — which release proteolytic enzymes (matrix metalloproteinases) that digest the basement membrane.
  4. Result: the epidermis lifts off the dermis → a subepidermal split → fluid accumulates → **tense blister**.
- **WHY each clinical sign happens:**
  - *Tense bullae* → Split below the epidermis; the roof is full-thickness epidermis which is strong, so blisters don't rupture easily (contrast with pemphigus where split is intraepidermal → fragile blisters).
  - *Pruritus* → Eosinophil degranulation releases histamine, eosinophil peroxidase, and other mediators that stimulate itch nerves. Often pruritus precedes blisters because immune activation begins before basement membrane cleavage.
  - *Urticarial plaques* → Complement-driven inflammation with vasodilation and edema in the dermis; these are "pre-bullous" zones where the split hasn't fully developed.
  - *Flexural distribution* → Not fully understood, but areas of friction/pressure may trigger blister formation in pre-sensitized skin. Also, lower body temperature in flexures may slow healing.
  - *Rarity of mucosal lesions* → Mucosal hemidesmosomes contain similar proteins but with slightly different isoforms (e.g., BP180 is present but may be less accessible to antibodies due to different splice variants). Also, the oral mucosa has faster healing and different immune milieu.

- **Connect pathophys to pattern recognition cues:**
  - Patient's pruritus before blisters → prodromal phase due to early eosinophil infiltration.
  - Tense blisters that don't pop → subepidermal split.
  - Surrounding urticarial plaques → complement activation and dermal edema.
  - No mucosal involvement → typical of BP; if present, think of mucous membrane pemphigoid instead.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Causes / Triggers | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common, >80%) | No identifiable trigger | "Elderly with no new medications" |
| **Drug-induced** | Diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide), ACE inhibitors (captopril, lisinopril), penicillamine, antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins), NSAIDs | "Blistering started 2–4 weeks after starting new drug" |
| **Neurological association** | Parkinson's disease, dementia, multiple sclerosis, stroke, epilepsy | "Elderly with bradykinesia, tremor, or cognitive decline" |
| **Physical triggers** | UV radiation, radiotherapy, trauma, burns | "Blisters confined to irradiated skin field" |
| **Viral** | Rare: HSV, VZV, HHV-8 (implicated in a few cases) | "Blistering erupted after oral herpes outbreak" |
| **Vaccination** | Influenza, pneumococcal (very rare case reports) | "Onset within 2 weeks of vaccination" |

- **Most common cause:** Idiopathic — no trigger found in the vast majority. Drug-induced is the most important to screen for because withdrawal can lead to resolution.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single validated scoring system in clinical use; diagnosis relies on **clinical picture + histology + immunofluorescence**. The combination is gold standard.
- **For each test:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Skin biopsy for histology** (punch biopsy of *new* blister, including edge) | Subepidermal split with eosinophil-rich infiltrate in the dermis (eosinophilic spongiosis) | Confirm level of blister and presence of eosinophils — key distinguishing feature from pemphigus (intraepidermal split) and linear IgA (neutrophil-rich) | Subepidermal split + eosinophils = consistent with BP; if neutrophils dominate, think linear IgA or dermatitis herpetiformis |
| **Direct immunofluorescence (DIF)** on perilesional skin (normal-appearing skin within 1 cm of blister) | Linear deposition of IgG and C3 along the dermal-epidermal junction | Definitive diagnostic test: shows where the immune attack is happening | Linear IgG + C3 at basement membrane = diagnostic of bullous pemphigoid (or mucous membrane pemphigoid if mucosa involved) |
| **Indirect immunofluorescence (IIF)** on monkey esophagus or salt-split skin (patient serum tested) | Circulating anti-basement membrane antibodies | Confirm active disease; also used to monitor response to therapy; salt-split skin shows antibody binding to *epidermal* side (roof) | Positive on epidermal side = BP; binding to dermal side = epidermolysis bullosa acquisita |
| **ELISA for BP180 and BP230** | Quantify autoantibody levels (anti-BP180 NC16A and anti-BP230) | Highly sensitive (90%+) and specific; aids diagnosis and predicts relapse | High titers correlate with active disease; falling titers with treatment; rising titers predict flare |
| **Full blood count** | Eosinophilia (often >0.5 ×10⁹/L) | Eosinophils are effector cells; high counts correlate with disease activity | Eosinophilia + tense blisters = strongly suggestive of BP |
| **Skin swab for culture** | Infecting organism if blister fluid purulent or surrounding cellulitis | Rule out secondary infection (Staph. aureus, Group A Strep) before starting high-dose steroids | Positive culture should prompt antibiotic therapy |

- **Single finding that confirms:** **Linear IgG and C3 along the dermal-epidermal junction on DIF** — this is pathognomonic.
- **Single finding that rules out:** **Intraepidermal split (acantholysis) on histology** → pemphigus vulgaris, not pemphigoid.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management depends on extent and severity. Use the **modified BP Disease Area Index (BPDAI)** to guide initial therapy:
- **Mild:** <10% body surface area (BSA) blisters/erosions; can use topical steroids.
- **Moderate:** 10–30% BSA; need systemic therapy.
- **Severe:** >30% BSA or rapid progression; high-dose systemic steroids + steroid-sparing agent.

- **Immediate (first hour):**
  - **What:** Assess airway, breathing, circulation. Is the patient septic? Any mucosal involvement? Check for Nikolsky sign (lateral pressure on blister → extension in pemphigus, NOT in BP). Obtain urgent skin biopsy (punch of new blister + perilesional for DIF). Send FBC, U&Es, LFTs, CRP, BP180 ELISA.
  - **Why:** Rule out SJS/TEN (Nikolsky positive, target lesions, systemic toxicity). Biopsy before starting steroids (steroids can clear DIF). Baseline bloods to check for drug-induced causes and monitor later.
  - **Doses:** None yet — focus on diagnosis and assessment.

- **Ward (24–72h):**
  - **Mild disease:** **Topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream** applied twice daily to affected areas, including surrounding erythema, for 2–4 weeks. This is as effective as systemic steroids in mild-moderate BP with fewer side effects. If refractory, add **doxycycline 100 mg PO BID** plus **nicotinamide 500 mg PO TID** — this combination has good evidence (BLISTER trial) and fewer side effects than prednisolone.
  - **Moderate–severe:** **Prednisolone** starting dose 0.5–1 mg/kg/day PO (max 80 mg/day) for 2–4 weeks, then taper slowly (by 5–10 mg every 2 weeks) aiming for maintenance ≤10 mg/day. Higher doses (1 mg/kg) for severe but start low if possible to reduce toxicity.
  - **Why prednisolone?** Rapidly suppresses inflammation and antibody production. Taper slowly because disease flares on fast tapering.
  - **Steoid-sparing agent** added early in moderate-severe disease: **Mycophenolate mofetil** 500–1000 mg BID PO or **Azathioprine** 1–2 mg/kg/day PO (check TPMT level first). These take 4–8 weeks to work, so don't stop prednisolone early.
  - **Wound care:** Puncture and drain large blisters aseptically (keep roof intact as biological dressing). Apply paraffin gauze (Jelonet) to eroded areas. Topical antimicrobials (silver sulfadiazine) if signs of infection.
  - **Monitoring:** Daily skin assessment for new blisters, signs of infection (purulent discharge, surrounding erythema, fever); FBC, CRP, U&Es weekly.

- **Long-term:**
  - **What:** Taper prednisolone as above; maintain steroid-sparing agent for 6–12 months. Once disease controlled (no new blisters for 2–4 weeks), slowly taper off immunosuppressants.
  - **Why:** BP is chronic but self-limiting in many; average duration 2–5 years. Relapse common during taper, so do it slowly. Continue topical steroids as needed for minor flares.
  - **Doses:** Maintenance prednisolone 2.5–5 mg/day; mycophenolate mofetil 500–1000 mg BID.
  - **Fallback:** **Rituximab** (1 g IV day 1 and day 15) for refractory disease; blocking B cells reduces antibody production.
  - **Prevent osteoporosis:** All patients on >7.5 mg/day prednisolone for >3 months need calcium/vitamin D supplements and bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate 70 mg once weekly).

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - **Don't start high-dose prednisolone before confirming diagnosis** — if it's SJS/TEN, steroids may worsen outcome. If it's drug-induced BP, withdrawing the drug may be enough.
  - **Don't ignore oral lesions** — if present, consider mucous membrane pemphigoid or pemphigus; need ophthalmology referral for conjunctival involvement.
  - **Don't use systemic steroids alone for mild disease** — the BLISTER trial showed topical clobetasol is as effective and safer.
  - **Don't forget to check for underlying malignancy** — only if atypical (young age, recalcitrant disease, associated with paraneoplastic pemphigus? Actually BP is NOT typically paraneoplastic; screening not routinely recommended unless red flags).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Bullous Pemphigoid | Pemphigus Vulgaris | Dermatitis Herpetiformis | Linear IgA Disease | Epidermolysis Bullosa Acquisita |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Blisters** | Tense, intact easily | Flaccid, fragile, easily ruptured | Small tense blisters on extensor surfaces | Tense blisters, often annular, "string of pearls" | Tense, trauma-prone (milia, scarring) |
| **Mucosal involvement** | Rare (<20%, mild) | Near-universal (oral, genital, conjunctival) | None (oral lesions rare) | Oral / ocular / genital in 50% | Oral and esophageal common |
| **Pruritus** | Intense, often pre-bullous | Mild or absent | Extremely intense, burning | Variable, can be pruritic | Mild |
| **Nikolsky sign** | Negative (blister doesn't extend) | Positive (lateral pressure extends blister) | Negative | Negative | Negative (occasionally positive in scarred areas) |
| **Histology** | Subepidermal split, eosinophils | Intraepidermal split (acantholysis), neutrophils/ eosinophils | Subepidermal microabscesses with neutrophils at dermal papillae | Subepidermal with neutrophils | Subepidermal, neutrophils, fibrosis |
| **Direct immunofluorescence** | Linear IgG + C3 at basement membrane | Intercellular (fishnet) IgG ± C3 in epidermis | Granular IgA in dermal papillae | Linear IgA at basement membrane | Linear IgG + C3 at basement membrane (dermal side on salt-split) |
| **Associated condition** | Parkinson's, dementia, drug-induced | Mostly idiopathic, some drugs | Gluten-sensitive enteropathy (celiac) | Vancomycin, other drugs; idiopathic | Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's) |
| **Key differentiating clue** | Pruritus + tense blisters + flexural distribution + elderly | Painful oral erosions + flaccid skin blisters | Severe itch + gluten sensitivity + extensor distribution | "String of pearls" annular lesions + drug history | Trauma-induced blisters + milia + scarring + IBD |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Secondary bacterial infection (most common):** Pustules, spreading erythema, purulent exudate, fever. **What to do:** skin swab, start empiric antibiotics (flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS PO) and adjust based on culture. If neglected → cellulitis, sepsis, bacteraemia.
- **Sepsis:** Widespread skin breakdown → loss of barrier → bacteria enter bloodstream. **Signs:** Fever, tachycardia, hypotension, high NEWS2, raised lactate. **What to do:** IV fluids, urgent blood cultures, IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2 g TDS or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe), escalate to medical team, consider ITU if septic shock.
- **Fluid and electrolyte imbalance:** Large eroded surfaces lose protein-rich exudate → hypoalbuminaemia, hypoproteinaemic edema. **Signs:** Pitting edema, low albumin (<25 g/L). **What to do:** monitor albumin, replace with IV fluids if needed (crystalloid), consider nutritional support (high protein). Rare unless >30% BSA.
- **Steroid-related complications (iatrogenic):** Hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, avascular necrosis, cataracts, immunosuppression → opportunistic infections (PCP, CMV, TB) if high-dose prolonged. **Prevention:** check blood glucose, start oral hypoglycaemics if needed; PCP prophylaxis (co-trimoxazole 480 mg OD) if prednisolone >20 mg/day for >1 month; bone protection as above.
- **Late presentation if untreated:** Months of severe pruritus and widespread blisters → chronic wounds → contractures, scarring (unusual but possible), depression, social withdrawal. In rare cases, bullous pemphigoid can blister extensively enough to be fatal from sepsis/multi-organ failure.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

- **Mnemonic for classic features: "BP in the ELDERLY"**
  - **E** = Elderly (age >70)
  - **L** = Linear IgG + C3 on DIF
  - **D** = Dermal-epidermal split (subepidermal)
  - **E** = Eosinophils on histology
  - **R** = Rare mucosal involvement
  - **L** = Long pruritic prodrome
  - **Y** = Yes, treat with steroids

- **Mnemonic for triggers: "DAN UP"**
  - **D** = Drugs (diuretics, ACEi, penicillamine)
  - **A** = Associated neurological (Parkinson's, dementia)
  - **N** = Nothing (idiopathic, most common)
  - **U** = UV / radiation
  - **P** = Physical trauma

- **High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
  1. Any elderly patient with unexplained itch >2 weeks → think pre-bullous pemphigoid. Do a skin biopsy for DIF even if no blisters.
  2. Tense blister + flexural distribution + eosinophilia = bullous pemphigoid until proven otherwise.
  3. Oral involvement is rare; if present and severe, reconsider diagnosis (pemphigus or mucous membrane pemphigoid).
  4. The "string of pearls" sign (annular grouping of blisters) is classic for **linear IgA disease**, not bullous pemphigoid — don't confuse them.
  5. **Doxycycline + nicotinamide** is a safe, steroid-sparing alternative to systemic steroids for mild-moderate BP — recommend it frequently to avoid steroid side effects in elderly.
  6. Relapse is common during first 6 months after tapering; do not stop all treatment too soon. Consider slow taper over 12 months.
  7. Monitoring anti-BP180 ELISA titers can predict relapse: a 4-fold drop correlates with remission; a rise precedes flare by 4–6 weeks.

- **Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
  - Mistaking Nikolsky-positive pemphigus for BP → delays correct treatment (pemphigus needs higher-dose steroids + steroid-sparing early).
  - Starting systemic steroids before DIF results → can clear immune deposits and make diagnosis impossible.
  - Using only topical steroids on severe disease → progression to >30% BSA and sepsis.
  - Not checking for underlying drug cause → persisting trigger until drug removed.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Condition | When to Call Senior / ICU | Specific Thresholds |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Widespread involvement (>30% BSA)** | Call dermatology registrar / consultant for joint management | Blisters cover >30% of body surface area; risk of sepsis, fluid loss, hypercatabolism |
| **Suspected sepsis** | Call medical registrar / sepsis team; consider ITU | **NEWS2 ≥ 5** or **lactate > 2 mmol/L**; fever >38.5°C or hypothermia <36°C; systolic BP <90 mmHg; new confusion |
| **Mucosal involvement with respiratory / ophthalmic signs** | Call ENT/ophthalmology and senior medics | Hoarseness, stridor, dysphagia → airway compromise; conjunctival injection, synechiae → blindness risk |
| **Poor response to first-line therapy** (no improvement after 2 weeks of prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg) | Call dermatology consultant for second-line options | Persistent new blister formation despite therapy; rising anti-BP180 titers |
| **Steroid toxicity** | Endocrine / rheumatology input | Random blood glucose >15 mmol/L (steroid-induced diabetes); new avascular necrosis (hip pain); active tuberculosis/reactivation of latent infection |
| **Anaphylaxis / drug reaction to treatment** | Immediate crash team call | Angioedema, stridor, hypotension, urticaria following rituximab or antibiotic; treat with 0.5 mg IM adrenaline |

- **Key numerical thresholds to remember:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** → call for urgent review; likely sepsis in this context.
  - **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** → tissue hypoperfusion; escalate to ITU if >4.
  - **GCS < 13** or new confusion → possible cerebral oedema from sepsis or steroid-induced hyperglycaemia; call senior.
  - **Platelets < 50 × 10⁹/L** or **INR > 1.5** → possible DIC from sepsis; call haematology and ITU.

---

*Final thought: Bullous pemphigoid is a great "syndrome" to master because it teaches you how to think about autoimmune blistering diseases: pattern of blister, depth of split, immunofluorescence, and age. Always ask yourself: "Could this be drug-induced?" Then treat the skin while protecting the patient from steroids.*
